facts.statement: A person who will live in the Netherlands for longer than 4 months must register as a resident in the Personal Records Database (BRP) with the municipality where they live.
A new resident must register within 5 days of arrival; accompanying partner and children must attend, and the municipality assigns a BSN through registration.
Resident registration is at the permanent home address or, when the legal conditions are met, a correspondence address.
BRP registration can require proof of lawful residence and identity verification; extra investigation or evidence may be necessary.

A person who lives outside the Netherlands or will stay in the Netherlands for less than 4 months uses the Non-residents Records Database (RNI), which is part of the BRP, and receives a BSN on registration.

RNI desk registration is in person, free of charge, and requires an eligible desk plus a valid passport or identity card; the current national page lists 19 desks and restricts non-Dutch, non-EU/EEA/Swiss applicants to Breda or Venlo.

For a child under 16, the RNI desk also requires the child's valid passport or identity card and a birth certificate or acknowledgement-of-parentage document; foreign documents may need translation or legalisation.

For Amsterdam only, a person moving from abroad for more than 4 months must register with the City within 5 days and bring the locally specified identity, occupancy and civil-status evidence that applies to their case.
Amsterdam's current first-registration page says registration is in person at a City Office, without an appointment except in Weesp; students follow a separate pre-registration instruction.

Amsterdam's current RNI route requires an appointment and eligible European passport or identity card, and says the BSN is issued immediately; applicants without an eligible European document are directed to Breda or Venlo.

facts.statement: A BSN is a unique personal number automatically allocated to a person registered in the BRP.

Residents receive a BSN through municipal BRP registration; eligible non-residents receive one through RNI registration, with limited alternative government-organisation routes for specific situations.
Someone who previously lived, worked or studied in the Netherlands may already have a BSN and should check before trying to register for another one.
For the ordinary resident and RNI routes, the BSN is the result of registration rather than a separate standalone BSN application.

A person who lives outside the Netherlands or will stay in the Netherlands for less than 4 months uses the Non-residents Records Database (RNI), which is part of the BRP, and receives a BSN on registration.

RNI desk registration is in person, free of charge, and requires an eligible desk plus a valid passport or identity card; the current national page lists 19 desks and restricts non-Dutch, non-EU/EEA/Swiss applicants to Breda or Venlo.

For Amsterdam only, a person moving from abroad for more than 4 months must register with the City within 5 days and bring the locally specified identity, occupancy and civil-status evidence that applies to their case.

Amsterdam's current RNI route requires an appointment and eligible European passport or identity card, and says the BSN is issued immediately; applicants without an eligible European document are directed to Breda or Venlo.

facts.statement: DigiD is used to identify oneself when arranging online matters with participating government, education, healthcare and pension organisations.
The standard Netherlands-resident DigiD application requires a BSN, an address registered with a Dutch municipality, and a mobile phone.
For the standard resident route, DigiD sends an activation code by post to the address recorded in the BRP; the detailed procedure says to allow up to 5 working days.
The DigiD activation code must be used within 21 days; if it expires, the user must apply again.
DigiD is personal and must not be applied for or shared on another person's behalf; DigiD Authorisation is the official route for delegated help.

The abroad application route requires a BSN, RNI registration, a valid passport or identity card, a phone that can receive Dutch SMS messages abroad, and an email address; activation then depends on the available official route.

facts.statement: Registering with a family doctor is not mandatory, but the Government of the Netherlands recommends doing so so that care is accessible when needed.
The Government of the Netherlands directs people who need help finding a family doctor to their municipality or health insurer.

facts.caveat: This is a guideline described by Zorginstituut, not a guarantee that a particular practice accepts a patient.
facts.statement: Zorginstituut Nederland says a GP has a guideline to be able to reach a patient for a home visit within 15 minutes and advises choosing a nearby practice.

When a provider has an excessive wait or announces a patient stop, patients can ask their health insurer for care mediation; the provider must point patients to this option in the situations identified by the NZa.

facts.caveat: The insurer's policy conditions determine provider and reimbursement details.
facts.statement: GP care and practice support are covered by the basic package and GP care itself is not subject to the compulsory deductible; prescribed medicines, tests performed outside the practice and referred care may be subject to the deductible.

facts.caveat: The same source identifies exceptions, including some foreign-employer, EU-institution, student and asylum situations; the duty must not be stated without those qualifications.
facts.statement: A person who comes to live or work in the Netherlands and is subject to the Dutch insurance duty must arrange Dutch health insurance as soon as possible and no later than four months after arrival, with coverage from the date of arrival.
The standard health insurance package is compulsory for people subject to the Dutch insurance duty; supplementary insurance is optional and is arranged with an insurer.

facts.caveat: The amount is calendar-year specific and must be reverified for later years. A personal contribution is a separate concept.
facts.statement: For 2026 the compulsory deductible is €385 for insured people aged 18 or over, while listed care including GP and out-of-hours GP care is exempt from the compulsory deductible.

After a CAK uninsured letter, the recipient must either take out Dutch basic insurance if required or request an SVB Wlz assessment if they believe the duty does not apply; doing neither within three months leads to a CAK fine.

facts.caveat: Highly volatile annual amount; never carry it into a later year without reverification.
facts.statement: The CAK page states that the uninsured fine is €529.74 in 2026 and that this amount is indexed annually.

facts.caveat: Rent thresholds and point values are year-specific and must be reverified when displayed.
facts.statement: Before accepting a rental, determine whether it is social, midprice or private-sector housing because different rules apply, and use the Huurcommissie Rent Check that matches the accommodation type.
For tenancy agreements dated 1 July 2023 or later, the maximum deposit described by Government.nl is two months' basic rent.

Tenancy agreements dated 1 July 2023 or later must be in writing, and the landlord must provide specified written information about use, access, protection, repairs, contacts, deposit and service costs.

A tenancy agreement should distinguish basic rent from service costs and record the parties, address, property description, start date, payment terms, deposit and signatures; the property's condition should be recorded in writing at the start.
An agent acting for the landlord may not also charge the tenant a mediation fee; a tenant who independently instructs an agent to find a home may owe that agent's fee.
Some municipalities require landlords to have a rental permit and can attach conditions, so the municipality must be checked for the specific address.

facts.statement: First report poor maintenance to the landlord by email or letter, list the defects, ask for repair within six weeks and keep a copy; request urgency when the problem is urgent.

For the Huurcommissie maintenance-defect procedure, the landlord has six weeks after notification to remedy the defects; if they do not, the tenant can submit a request and must provide dated evidence of the notification.

facts.caveat: The sector and transition rules are legally detailed and should be determined before presenting a specific route.
facts.statement: Huurcommissie authority differs by rental sector and contract date; for a private-sector home it can provide only advice when both parties agreed in writing to submit the dispute, while social and qualifying midprice cases can receive decisions under the stated rules.

A tenant can also report poor maintenance to the municipality; if the municipality finds the home violates the Building Works Living Environment Decree, it can order the landlord to repair and can arrange the work at the landlord's cost if the order is ignored.

facts.caveat: This reporting point is not automatically the correct enforcement route for every physical repair defect.
facts.statement: Every municipality has a reporting point for unwanted landlord conduct under the Good Landlordship Act, including missing written agreements or information, unjustified service or deposit costs, discrimination and intimidation.

facts.statement: 9292 can plan public-transport journeys and shows planned disruptions; because timetables and disruptions can change, its guidance says to check the route shortly before departure for the correct date and time.

facts.caveat: This is planner behavior, not a guarantee that every facility is operational at travel time.
facts.statement: The 9292 planner has an accessible-journey option that can add transfer time and route via stops and stations marked accessible.
For occasional travel, NS states that a valid contactless debit card, credit card or mobile device can be used without prior activation; the same card or device must be used to check out.
Check out with the operator used to check in; a change to another train operator can require checking out and checking in again.
NS describes debit-card travel as second class without a discount and advises season-ticket holders to continue using the product and card associated with their season ticket.

facts.statement: EURES helps jobseekers find work in EU/EEA countries and Switzerland, and UWV is the coordinating office for EURES in the Netherlands.

facts.caveat: Access requires the appropriate DigiD or recognised European electronic identity route described by UWV.
facts.statement: UWV's Work Folder can be used to post a CV, search vacancies and access courses or webinars; benefit recipients can also report job-search activities there.

facts.caveat: This fact applies only to the PD U2 benefit-export scenario, not to every jobseeker.
facts.statement: A person who comes to the Netherlands to seek work while exporting an unemployment benefit from another EU/EEA country or Switzerland must obtain PD U2 from the foreign benefit body and register as a jobseeker with UWV by telephone within seven days.

facts.caveat: These obligations are for applicable benefit recipients and must never be presented as a legal duty for all jobseekers.
facts.statement: For people who are subject to UWV's unemployment-benefit job-search requirement, recognised activities include applications, interviews, posting a CV, registering with an agency, employer contact, networking and specified workshops, and evidence should be retained for up to two years.

facts.statement: Within one week after employment starts, the employer must provide specified information in writing, including the parties, workplace, role, start date, fixed-term duration where relevant, working hours, salary/payment timing and any probation length.

Within one month after employment starts, the employer must provide further specified written information, including holiday allowance, holidays, notice period and any applicable pension, non-compete clause and collective agreement.
An employment contract can be oral or written, but Rijksoverheid advises recording it in writing so rights and duties are fixed clearly.

A fixed-term contract normally ends automatically at its end or project completion and usually cannot be ended early unless this was agreed; a permanent contract continues until employee or employer ends it under the applicable rules.

An applicable collective labour agreement can govern pay and other terms and takes priority over an individual contract; the individual contract may not be less favourable than the collective agreement, while more favourable terms can be allowed.

facts.statement: A person invited by the Tax Administration to file an income tax return must file; a person without an invitation may still have to file or may file to request a refund.
The filing route depends on residence status and the tax year: people resident abroad for the full year and people who immigrated or emigrated during the year use different return routes.
The current Tax Administration page offers online, app and paper filing routes, with entrepreneurs generally required to file online and stated exceptions for entrepreneurs living abroad.
Tax partners each use their own DigiD; filing for someone else requires the official authorisation route rather than sharing credentials.
A resident with health insurance, rented housing or children may be eligible for a government contribution, but eligibility is determined separately for each allowance and is not automatic.
An allowance can be applied for through Mijn toeslagen using DigiD, and Dienst Toeslagen also offers telephone help and assisted appointments.
If the conditions are met, an allowance is paid monthly as an advance and is followed by a final calculation after the year; relevant changes must be reported and an excess may have to be repaid.

The current Dienst Toeslagen page says changes affecting the allowance must be reported through Mijn toeslagen within 4 weeks; a move already reported to the municipality does not need to be reported again to Dienst Toeslagen.
Tax thresholds, allowance amounts, income and asset limits, deadlines and cross-border treatment are tax-year- and situation-specific and must not be presented as permanent generic figures.

facts.statement: A bank decides whether to accept an application for an ordinary payment account and may ask about identity and the source of funds because it must prevent money laundering and terrorist financing.

facts.caveat: The DNB source does not say that every ordinary account application universally requires a BSN.
facts.statement: A bank must verify the customer's identity and may make or request a copy of an identity document; it may request other personal data, including a BSN when needed to meet tax-law obligations, and must explain why data is needed.

A consumer who cannot open an ordinary payment account can apply for a basic payment account, subject to statutory refusal grounds including KYC compliance and proof of a genuine connection to the Netherlands such as living, studying or working here.
A consumer who disagrees with a rejection can complain to the bank and, if dissatisfied with the response, may in many cases complain to Kifid or go to court.

facts.statement: Call 112 when urgent help is needed because life is in immediate danger, someone is seriously injured and needs prompt medical attention, or a serious crime is being witnessed.
When calling 112, say what happened, what help is needed and whether police, fire brigade or ambulance appears to be required.
If the regular GP practice is closed, contact the out-of-hours GP service before going to a hospital emergency department, except in a life-threatening situation, when 112 should be called directly.
GP and out-of-hours GP care do not consume the compulsory deductible; hospital emergency-department care is hospital care and is subject to the compulsory deductible.

facts.caveat: 0900-8844 is a police number, not a general medical advice line.
facts.statement: For police emergencies in the Netherlands use 112 free of charge; for non-emergency police questions or appointments use 0900-8844, and from abroad use +31 343 57 8844.

facts.statement: There is no single universal Dutch residence-permit application: the applicable IND route depends on the person's nationality, purpose of stay, sponsor and circumstances.
Citizens of the EU, EEA or Switzerland do not generally need a residence permit to stay in the Netherlands, although a specific prior-residency situation can require registration with the IND.
Requirements, documents, sponsor duties, costs and decision periods must be taken from the current IND page for the selected residence purpose; a generic guide must not merge the routes.
A residence document should be collected only after the IND has said it is ready, at the specific collection location stated in the message, and the holder must collect it in person.

The ordinary IND collection checklist includes a valid passport or travel document, the appointment code, and any existing or expired residence document; a police report is required if the prior document was lost or stolen.

For a valid residence document that is lost or stolen, the holder must report the loss or theft to the police, obtain a certified copy of the report, and include a copy with the replacement application.

If the document is lost or stolen abroad, the holder must report it to the local police; a report outside Dutch, English, French or German needs a sworn translation, and return travel may require a visa.
A residence document becomes invalid after the police report; if it is later found, it still must be handed in to the IND and a replacement is required.

If a lost or stolen regular residence permit has less than 3 months' validity remaining, the IND says an extension or change application can be filed instead of a replacement for that period, with the police report shown at collection.
The IND page for replacement after loss or theft states an 8-week decision period that can be extended and says a fee applies; the live fee page must be checked rather than storing a generic amount.

facts.statement: Before enrolling, verify that the institution is recognised by Dutch authorities and that the bachelor's or master's programme is accredited by NVAO.

facts.caveat: Some programmes permit later application and the institution decides admission; the institution's current deadline and requirements must always be checked.
facts.statement: For a first associate-degree or bachelor's application, the general deadline is 1 May via Studielink; a numerus-fixus programme has a 15 January deadline, while master's deadlines must be checked with the programme.
The university or university of applied sciences decides admission, and conditions can differ by institution and by programme.

facts.caveat: The amount is academic-year specific and must be reverified for another year.
facts.statement: Higher education has statutory and institutional tuition; the statutory tuition fee for academic year 2026-2027 is €2,694, while institutions set institutional tuition and eligibility for the statutory fee depends on the official criteria.

facts.caveat: Being an international student does not by itself establish eligibility.
facts.statement: Enrolled students may apply for student finance only if they meet nationality or equivalent-rights and other eligibility conditions; its components can include grants, a travel product and loans, and student finance is not always a gift.

facts.caveat: These are routes to search, not verified listings or a guarantee of availability.
facts.statement: Rijksoverheid identifies housing associations that offer student rooms and private landlords, advertisements, agents and personal networks as possible room-search routes.
After finding a room, the tenant should have a tenancy agreement recording rent, termination and other agreements and must report the new address to the municipality on time.
A housing permit may be required for a room depending on the situation and municipality.
Shared accommodation is treated as social housing with a rent ceiling in the Government.nl tenant plan, and the Huurcommissie offers a Rent Check specifically for shared accommodation.
Student rooms remain subject to the ordinary tenant checks for written agreements, deposit, service costs and landlord conduct described in the national tenant plan.

facts.statement: A change of address within the Netherlands can be reported from 4 weeks before the move until 5 days after it, to the municipality of the new address.

If a change is reported more than 5 days after the move, the report date can become the official move date; a municipality may impose a fine of up to EUR 325 for failing to report important personal-data changes in time.

The national baseline documents are valid identity evidence and proof of occupancy, such as a rental contract, purchase contract or consent from the main occupant; the receiving municipality may specify the exact upload or form.
When moving to another Dutch municipality, the resident reports to the new municipality; the new municipality updates the BRP and the previous municipality deregisters the resident.

Municipal social-support or social-assistance services do not necessarily transfer automatically, so the resident should ask the new municipality what remains available and whether a new application is needed.

For a foreign national with a residence permit who reports an in-country move on time, the municipality forwards the new address to the IND; if the municipal report is late, the person must also notify the IND.
Amsterdam accepts its listed online, City Office and postal routes with locally specified occupancy evidence and currently states processing within 3 weeks after the move date.
A person moving from Amsterdam to another Dutch municipality reports the move to the new municipality, which triggers deregistration from Amsterdam.

facts.statement: The official national start-up plan is a general guideline; the founder must identify additional rules for their specific immigration status, sector, location, staff, products and starting situation.

KVK currently says registration is required when the person independently provides or prepares to provide products or services, charges for income, and regularly supplies people beyond family and friends; borderline cases require KVK assessment.

A founder must choose a legal structure because it affects liability, tax and registration; a legal entity such as a BV or NV is registered through a civil-law notary, and 'zzp' or 'freelancer' is not itself a legal structure.
A founder from outside the EU, EEA or Switzerland may need an appropriate residence permit to stay and start a business and must use the IND route for their situation.

The ordinary Dutch KVK route requires a BSN and a Dutch business address; an RNI-registered non-resident must also be able to prove the permanent foreign address and temporary Netherlands address described by the official page.

For the ordinary self-registration route, the founder completes the KVK registration form, makes an appointment, attends with valid identity evidence, and brings any address or other documents listed in the confirmation.

KVK says a business should be registered 1 week before or after activities start; details may be checked up to 3 months before the start date, with final registration taking effect near the start date.
KVK passes ordinary Dutch registration details to the Tax Administration, but KVK's entrepreneur criteria and the Tax Administration's tax criteria differ; tax status is communicated separately.
The one-off KVK fee for a new registration is EUR 85.15 in 2026; KVK updates the fee annually, and optional extracts or notarial costs are separate.

Before opening, a founder must check which permits, registrations, professional requirements and location rules apply to the actual activity; the generic start-up plan cannot answer this without sector and location data.
The Business Register contains public business information; the founder should review the current KVK rules for shielding a visiting address before using a private home address.
